risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "We used a computer-generated randomization list that stratified assignment into block sizes of six and six strata based upon study site and participant use (Y/N) of an oral contraceptive." Comment: This explicitly describes a computer-generated random allocation sequence with stratification and block randomization, which indicates a proper random method was used.

_evidence.method_quote: We used a computer-generated randomization list that stratified assignment into block sizes of six and six strata based upon study site and participant use (Y/N) of an oral contraceptive.
_evidence.method_summary: Computer-generated randomization list with stratification by study site and oral contraceptive use, using block sizes of six.
context_clues: participant use (Y/N) of an oral contraceptive

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A research assistant (RA) who had no contact with participants prepared sequentially numbered stock bottles that had no information about the test drug. Sites were sent two lists (Y/N oral contraceptives) specific for their center, and stock bottles. The RA took stock bottles in sequence from the appropriate list and filled a smaller bottle with a medication event monitoring system (MEMS) cap that was given to the participant." Comment: This describes a concealment mechanism that prevented foreknowledge by the recruiter: the person preparing the assignments had no participant contact, and participants received sequentially numbered stock bottles with no information about the test drug. Although not phrased as central allocation or SNOSE, it is an explicit other mechanism that appears to prevent the enrolling staff from predicting the next assignment.

_evidence.concealment_quote: A research assistant (RA) who had no contact with participants prepared sequentially numbered stock bottles that had no information about the test drug. Sites were sent two lists (Y/N oral contraceptives) specific for their center, and stock bottles. The RA took stock bottles in sequence from the appropriate list and filled a smaller bottle with a medication event monitoring system (MEMS) cap that was given to the participant.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: “This was a double-blind, placebo-controlled, multi-site, parallel group trial” and “masking of study medication was maintained.” Comment: The study explicitly states that both participants and treatment personnel were blinded, with placebo control and maintained masking; therefore knowledge of allocation was unlikely to influence the predominantly subjective outcomes (e.g., PMTS, IDS-C, DRSP, CGI, self-reported symptom measures).

_evidence.blinding_quote: “This was a double-blind, placebo-controlled, multi-site, parallel group trial” and “masking of study medication was maintained.”
outcome_categories: number of symptomatic days before pills were taken
subjective_outcomes: number of symptomatic days before pills were taken

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Premenstrual symptom severity was captured through administration of the Premenstrual Tension Scale(PMTS) and the Inventory of Depressive Symptomatology-Clinician version (IDS-C)... A clinician assigned a Clinical Global Impression Severity (CGI-S) score... Follow-up visits... assigned CGI-Improvement (CGI-I) and CGI-S scores... Information on adverse events was collected at all visits." Comment: The outcome assessors included clinicians for PMTS, IDS-C, CGI-S/CGI-I and adverse events are subjective/judgement-dependent outcomes. Although the trial was described as double-blind and "Inter-rater reliability was maintained throughout the trial via videotapes," this does not explicitly state that outcome assessors were blinded, and the presence of multiple clinician-rated subjective outcomes means lack of clear assessor blinding could bias measurement. Patient-reported DRSP and Michelson outcomes were also collected, but the clinician-rated subjective outcomes alone make the detection-bias domain high risk under the worst-outcome rule.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: Inter-rater reliability was maintained throughout the trial via videotapes. The intra-class correlation coefficient (ICC) was at 0.8 or higher.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Overall, 75% of participants completed the trial or were moved to rescue treatment. Groups had similar retention, although more participants in the placebo (n=9) than the sertraline (n=3) group were moved to rescue treatment." Comment: The analysed primary/secondary repeated-measures outcomes used mixed models, with per-arm analysed numbers shown (e.g. baseline 125 vs 127; cycle 1 110 vs 123; cycle 2 104 vs 112; later cycles still balanced), giving total post-randomization loss of about 9.6% and no meaningful arm imbalance. Although some participants were removed for lack of adequate response and rescue treatment was not included in efficacy analyses, the attrition was low overall and balanced across arms, so it is unlikely to have biased the outcome results.

per_arm_flow.reasons: “Women who did not achieve a CGI-S rating of ≤2 after two months at 100mg or the highest tolerated dose were offered removal from the trial alongside daily sertraline ‘rescue’ treatment.” “more participants in the placebo (n=9) than the sertraline (n=3) group were moved to rescue treatment.”

“Women who did not achieve a CGI-S rating of ≤2 after two months at 100mg or the highest tolerated dose were offered removal from the trial alongside daily sertraline ‘rescue’ treatment.” “more participants in the placebo (n=9) than the sertraline (n=3) group were moved to rescue treatment.”

_evidence.missing_data_method: linear mixed effects models; generalized estimating equations; for responder and remission (CGI-I) analysis, “last visit carried forward”
_evidence.notes: CONSORT-style flow is partly reported via Table 2/Results. Primary continuous outcomes used all available repeated measures in mixed models; CGI-I responder/remission used LOCF. Rescue-treatment removals were excluded from efficacy analysis.
